speaker: interviewee
text: All right. Well, I came through Ellis Island. And my aunt was there. But through the HIAS, we were-- we had to go to a foster home. We couldn't go. I think what happened is my aunt perhaps didn't have a big apartment for us because they went to look her over-- to look over the situation and so on. So they assigned a family to me, lived in Queens for a while. And then within a month, my aunt got another apartment and I went to live with her. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us again your aunt's name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My aunt's name was Ann Goder, or Assia, we used to call her. Or I used to call her [RUSSIAN], which means aunt in Russian. She was working at United Nations and she always had a very responsible in the-- in the accounting department. My uncle was an engineer. And they had no children, so, you know, they took a large apartment where I had my own bedroom, my cousin had my own bedroom, and they had their own bedroom. It was right on 10th Street in Manhattan, right between Fifth Avenue and University Place. It was a very nice apartment. My aunt unfortunately had never had any children. And she thought that she wanted to mold me in her image-- in other words, have the best for me and educate and try to bring me up in all my manners and so on, so forth. And she really didn't do it in a very diplomatic manner. But I wasn't a child anymore, whereas my cousin, Wolf, even though he was the same age, was, you know, took it from her. Well, she started to criticize me-- do this, do that. And I said, well, I really didn't like it. So we would get into arguments. Why don't you-- you know, when you live in my house, you have to do it this way. Well, I immediately found a second cousin who was a teacher in-- in Brooklyn, in Thomas Jefferson High School. So she came over to see me and other relatives. I didn't have too many relatives there in New York. But they came over, and they-- and this teacher-- this cousin said, why don't you-- I will talk to the principal and you can go to the school and learn English because I didn't speak a word of English. And so I went there, took the train, and went over there on the subway. And within-- this was in January. And by June, I said that I-- I was going to graduate from high school and I was going to enroll in college-- Hunter College. I passed the region's American-- American history. In New York, before you enter college, you have to pass Regents in-- in English and political-- or history. And I passed them both and I-- I went on my own and-- and went to Hunter College and joined also, went through. HIAS was very nice in-- in helping Jewish children especially. This was-- I came to the United States in December, 1946. So this was January 1947 when I entered Thomas Jefferson. In June, July-- in June, I took my Regents. In July, I started looking into going to Hunter College. And they helped me, the HIAS. And HIAS said that they would give me a scholarship for $100. And in fact, they were going to-- I-- and I was very unhappy living with my aunt.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they said they would find me a place to live. And-- but I didn't want that. So I said, well, I found myself a job through-- I entered college.

speaker: interviewee
text: And within a couple months, I even have-- had enough chutzpah, guts, to say that I want to use credit for my gymnasium finishing. And all the papers were gone. So I took German and so on and so forth-- advanced. And-- and they passed me. So they said, all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went-- I struggled. It was a hard life, but I did it. That was my aim. I graduated from college in 1940-- 1950-- February 1950-- no, June 1950 after-- so I was just barely 20 years old and-- in chemistry. Were you supporting yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was supporting wholly myself. I had a scholarship for $100 a month and I worked as a tutor for a child. And thereby, I got my room and board. And I did babysitting and worked in the chemistry department in school in-- in the Hunter College and graduated with a bachelor-- with a science degree in chemistry. And then I was going to go on to medical school. Well, I had met my husband-- who was later on my husband. He was-- I was just barely 16. He was 16 too. And he was helping me a great deal with my English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he American?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he was-- well, no, he was actually from Vienna. And he-- so he was always helping me with my homework and everything was very bright. He is very bright and helping me with science and math because I-- I had so many lags in my education that I had to catch up on everything. And here I was taking algebra, you know, and trigonometry and all this. Anyhow, I did it and then applied to go to medical school. But we decided instead to get married. It was a hard life for me. But I did it, you know. I, again, approached everything. I had an aim, and this is what I was going to do. I learned that my family-- later on, I learned-- actually, when I was in the United States, I started getting letters from Israel. And I had a large family in Israel-- cousins and so on-- that my father was, within six months, he was sent to a labor camp and he died a horrible death. No one escaped from that labor camp. He was-- at the time, he was like 40.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was a Russian labor camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Russian labor camp-- my mother and my two sisters were in Siberia. My aunt-- my uncle Leiba, that was his brother. And he was instrumental in bringing my aunt, my mother, and two sisters from Siberia. And my mother was a-- it's a long story. My mother had started a-- a growth on her breasts which she went to the hospital, Russian hospital, and begged them to operate. And they said, well, it's nothing wrong with you. It's not malignant. It's-- you know, so they sent her back. And by that time, after six, eight months, it grew. It grew, and it was already out of hand. My uncle came over. She didn't know what she had because they tried to tell her that she-- you know, it was benign. And my uncle came to visit her in Siberia and immediately made papers to-- for her to come back to Lithuania. This was like 1958. And she-- they took her to the hospital and-- and they opened her up and they closed her up. It was just too far gone. So here were my sisters, two sisters. And neither one-- they had made papers through a fictitious marriage with a Pole to go-- to leave Lithuania. But unfortunately, mother was sick and neither one of them wanted to leave. And my mother was like-- again, like a miracle happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: She-- one day, she knew that they-- they were held up because of her. And she says, you know what? I'm fine. Dress me, I'm going.

speaker: interviewee
text: She-- they were waiting for her to die, and she got enough strength together to go by taxi to the train station and to go to Poland. And from Poland, all the papers were quick and they went to Israel. And they arrived in Israel. She had blood transfusions. She went to the hospital. And I was waiting. Already I knew about it from my cousins in Israel who were corresponding with me. And so within a couple of weeks after their arrival there, I had a two-year-old. And I took Debbie, my oldest one. And incidentally, I didn't mention that I got married in between. In 1951, we got married and we came out to California. And in 1956, my daughter was born, Debbie. And so I took Debbie and I went to Israel. And I saw my mother after 18 years and my sisters. And I-- you know, we were there while my mother died six weeks later-- well, four weeks later, she died. I stayed on for another two weeks Israel. This was 1959. Israel was still under very, you know, primitive conditions. My sisters were established-- you know, got established there and one was a-- was an engineer. She had her schooling already in-- in Russia. So she was an engineer and she immediately got a very good job. And she's very bright and very organized and directed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she got established there and she married there and she has two children. The other one entered University Tel Aviv-- no, Technion actually first. Technion, and then she went in Haifa and then she went to University of Jerusalem. And she graduated in pharmacy. She was also a pharmacist. Well, I, you know, came back to California and-- Can-- can I ask you a philosophical question here? Just one small one-- how had-- does-- did this war experience and your being alone so much and having all these adventures, as you say, how have those things affected the rest of your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, of course, it left me with, I suppose, selfish outlook. It took-- it took me a long time to really integrate in the American way of living even though I immediately-- my way was, again, survival. And leaving everything behind and moving on and wanting to integrate, I stopped seeing everybody from-- as I used to call them-- I didn't want to be labeled a greener, which is, you know, the newcomers. And I wanted to assimilate, and I wanted to become part of the American way of culture. And my aunt, of course, was-- I-- I remained friends with my aunt even though I didn't live with her. And she became my best friend. And she really, you know, respected me for what I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so how did it affect me? Well, it undoubtedly psychologically, you know, it left me with scars. But I don't really think about it. I am-- I was always a very achiever. And I just knew that I wanted the best that I-- that I could have.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you can attain it if you want it. So that was my parents' philosophy of always hard work, and you can attain anything. And that was mine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you pass this along to your own children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. I really did. I always said-- you know, in fact, from the-- from-- from the time they were little, I-- I brought them up where you have to stand on your own two feet. Because I will be there. But if you want to eat, here is a refrigerator. Make yourself some food. Learn how to do everything in life. Because the more you arm yourself with the more education-- always education was of prime importance. To this day, I take classes. I never miss anything. And explore everything. And-- and I had a curiosity. Were my children-- I must say some-- there were times that they rebelled. And perhaps they were in rebellion. Now that they are-- two of them are married and have their own families. They realize that-- you know, that's-- that-- because my husband unfortunately had another philosophy. He-- he was very bright and very-- but he wasn't just as ambitious, I would say, in material outlook. I wanted the material not because my parents were-- spoiled me in any way. But parents said, you can do it if you want to. So-- and if you-- and unfortunately, you need money to do all these things, the better things in life. And so I always said, I wanted the best career I could have so that I could live-- afford to live a good life. He didn't. So we had arguments about it. And eventually, we were divorced. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many children did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have three children. One--

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have grandchildren now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I have-- my children now are 33, 39. And my daughter Debbie is-- Debbie Graham. She is-- she's going to be 39 in October. And my son was 33 in January. She's a school teacher. She was an art-- applied art major in UCLA. And she's a school teacher. And my son is a CPA. And he's married. He has one-- two children, actually. One is four. One is a baby six months old. And they're all very ambitious, and they all really do their own thing. And Paula, she is now 28. And she is into Jewish studies even though she rebelled for a great deal. But now she was really instrumental in saying, Mom, you've got to leave all this so we will remember so we'll have it. We'll know where we come from. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Really even now, I'm just-- OK, this is the brooch that I risked my life for when I left the ghetto to come back. It's hard for me to believe that, for that and for a-- for a few pictures, that I clung to what I had left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whose was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was my mother's pin. And I got it-- I went back to the house or my aunt went back after they were sent out to Siberia. And she found a few things. So when I came back, she says, this is your mother's brooch and-- and a few clothing and pictures.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your mother's full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's full name was Genia-- Genia Taft Ceitlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: That brooch.

speaker: interviewee
text: I would imagine that she got this probably in 1930-- in the late 1930s. This is my aunt Assia, who-- this was taken probably around 1921, '22-- who had left for Paris to study there. And she was the one who brought me to the United States and was very, very close to me. She died in 1959. Her name was Assia Goder, and she lived in New York on 10th Street. In fact, I remember-- 21 East 10th Street, where I lived with her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the place where the photograph was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the-- the place-- the photo was taken in Lithuania in Kovno. This is a-- a picture that was taken about 1931, '32. I will start from left to right. It was in a summer house where we used to vacation. This was our [NON-ENGLISH], our governess, housekeeper. And there I was at the age of two, two and a half, with my head shaven because that's how they felt hair grows in better. And then it was my grandmother whose name was Kaila Mary. And she was the one that I said who lived with us until 1939 when she died. And then my mother and my little sister Mina, who is in my mother's arms. This is a picture of the opening of a new wing in our store. I-- my-- I and my sister, Mina, who is now Mina Eibshitz in Israel, are around the flower basket. And then from left to right, this third one from the left is my father Mayer Zvi, Mayer Ceitlin, and then my mother Eugenia or Genia. And then most of them were-- all of them, in fact, were salespeople in the store.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was probably taken around 1934, '35, '36, probably. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the place is?

speaker: interviewee
text: And it-- and it was in [INAUDIBLE], where our store was on 40 [INAUDIBLE], which is a suburb of Kovno. This is a picture of my father, Mayer Ceitlin. He was [INAUDIBLE] Hasid. It was taken in Kovno. And I would say that it's about 1938, '39. He was a very handsome man. This is a picture of my two sisters. From left, the little one is Basia. She was married in Israel, Basia Fein. And she was here about two years old. And it was taken about 1940, 1940 in Kovno. And the one who is standing up is my sister Mina Eibshitz. And this was also, of course, in the same place, same time. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is a picture of my-- of myself, actually. It was right after we were liberated. I found these two friends in Kovno. And it was 1944. And one is Mita Schapps and-- the one who is standing. And I am right under her in the polka dot dress. And then the one on the right is Lotte Fineburg. Now, Mita Schapps went to Israel. Lotte Fineburg went to South Africa where there were many Lithuanian Jews. She found some relatives who were there. Here I am in [INAUDIBLE]. When I arrived there, of course, Bavaria.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they dressed me up as a Bavarian girl. This was in 1945-- 1946, actually, probably around March 1946 in [INAUDIBLE]. And that's me, Marcia Ceitlin Spies. What--

speaker: interviewee
text: It is in Bavaria, Germany. All right, this is a picture of my family in 1975. It was during my son Gene's bar mitzvah. The one on the left is Debbie. Her Hebrew name is [? Zvia ?] from my father. And here I am. And my son, who is the bar mitzvah boy, Eugene-- Eugene or Gene Edward-- Gene was named after my mother-- and then my husband, Robert Spies. And the one who is right under him is Paula Carolyn Spies. And of course, Paula was named after my Aunt Paula, who was very close to me.
